fuzzy_prd: We need a lightweight, reusable toolkit of pure helper functions for cleaning and interpreting web content without requiring network access. The library should cover several categories of operations: normalizing messy page text (collapsing extra whitespace, stripping trailing site-name suffixes from titles), classifying web addresses by the type of content they likely point to (video, audio, downloadable documents, archives, executables, or readable pages), and cleaning up URLs by following well-known link-forwarding interstitials from major platforms and stripping campaign analytics tags from query strings. The toolkit should also support extracting metadata from page markup fragments — specifically parsing icon size declarations to find the best high-resolution favicon, and pulling the single most representative image or link reference out of an arbitrary HTML subtree. All operations must be pure and deterministic given the same input, with no side effects or external calls. The I/O contract is a simple line-oriented key-value protocol where each result is emitted as one or more lines in the form "key=value", with booleans written as lowercase words. URL results must be returned in normalized form (a bare host URL gains a trailing slash). When classifying a URL, the system must output exactly seven boolean lines in a fixed order. The image extractor searches for content in a specific priority order — checking direct attributes on the root element before descending into the subtree and finally inspecting inline style declarations for background references.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object with op) -> stdout web-content utility result lines

trigger_keywords: what order are classify_url boolean fields emitted
what is the output format for URL classification
which lines does classify_url produce and in what sequence
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: classify_url emits exactly 7 boolean lines in this fixed order: video, image, audio, binary_document, archive, executable, article. Each is 'true' or 'false' lowercase. The 'article' line is true only when all six media-type flags are false.

trigger_keywords: what is the output key for whitespace normalization
how is the result of whitespace collapsing returned
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: clean_whitespace emits a single line: result=<normalized text>. All maximal runs of whitespace (spaces, tabs, newlines) are replaced by a single space and leading/trailing whitespace is trimmed. An all-whitespace input yields 'result=' (empty value).

trigger_keywords: what is the pipe rule for title cleaning
when does clean_title strip the site name
how does the pipe position affect title cleaning
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: clean_title checks: if the LAST position of '|' is greater than half the string length (lastIndexOf('|') > length / 2), it keeps only the text before the FIRST '|', trimmed of surrounding whitespace. Otherwise the title is returned with whitespace normalized (removeWhiteSpace). The midpoint comparison uses the total string length divided by 2.

trigger_keywords: which query param names are stripped by remove_tracking
what is the complete list of analytics tags that get deleted
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: remove_tracking removes any query parameter whose name is in the TRACKING_PARAMETERS set: _openstat, action_object_map, action_ref_map, action_type_map, fb_action_ids, fb_action_types, fb_ref, fb_source, ga_campaign, ga_content, ga_medium, ga_place, ga_source, ga_term, gs_l, utm_campaign, utm_cid, utm_content, utm_id, utm_medium, utm_name, utm_place, utm_pubreferrer, utm_reader, utm_source, utm_swu, utm_term, utm_userid, utm_viz_id, yclid. All other parameters are preserved.

trigger_keywords: what query parameter does Facebook rewriter use
how does rewrite_facebook detect a shim URL
what conditions trigger Facebook link unwrapping
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: rewrite_facebook unwraps only when the URL host ends with '.facebook.com' AND the encoded path equals exactly '/l.php'. The real destination is read from the 'u' query parameter. Unwrapping loops until the resolved URL no longer meets both conditions. Non-matching URLs are returned unchanged (normalized by URL parsing, so a bare host gains a trailing '/').

trigger_keywords: what query parameter does Google rewriter use
how does rewrite_google detect a redirect URL
what is the fallback parameter when q is absent for Google
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: rewrite_google unwraps only when the URL host ends with '.google.com' AND the encoded path equals exactly '/url'. The real destination is read from the 'q' query parameter; if 'q' is absent, it falls back to the 'url' query parameter. Unwrapping loops until the resolved URL no longer meets both conditions.

trigger_keywords: what is the output when no image is found by extract_image
how is an empty image result represented
what does extract_image output when the HTML has no image
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When no image is found, extract_image emits 'image_url=' (key present, value empty string after the equals sign). When no link is found, extract_link emits 'link_url=' similarly.

trigger_keywords: what is the image extraction priority order
how does the image extractor search for an image in the subtree
which attributes are checked first when extracting an image URL
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: ImageUrlExtractor.findImage() checks in this exact order: (1) 'src' attribute on the root element, (2) 'data-src' on root, (3) 'src' on descendant elements, (4) 'data-src' on descendant , (5) 'src' on any descendant element, (6) 'data-src' on any descendant, (7) CSS background-image url(...) in style attribute on elements with role=img, (8) CSS background-image on any element. The first non-blank match wins.

trigger_keywords: what does parse_size return for null input
what value is returned for unparseable size string
what is the sentinel value when sizes cannot be parsed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: parseSize returns 0 for null, blank, or unparseable input. For a valid 'WxH' pair it returns the MAX of the two dimensions (not the width alone). For multiple space-separated pairs it returns the max dimension across ALL pairs. The 'x' separator is case-insensitive.

trigger_keywords: what is the output key for URL operations
how are resolved or rewritten URLs printed to output
what line format does the URL rewriter produce
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All URL-returning operations (rewrite_facebook, rewrite_google, resolve_redirects, remove_tracking, find_largest_icon) emit a single line 'url=<normalized url>'. A host-only URL such as 'http://example.com' becomes 'http://example.com/' (trailing slash added by URL normalization).

trigger_keywords: what extension sets are classified as binary document
what formats are included in the binary document category
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: isLikelyBinaryDocument() returns true for: doc, pdf, ppt, rtf, swf, xls. Note that 'pdf' appears in BOTH the image and binary_document extension sets, so a .pdf URL will have both image=true and binary_document=true, and article=false.

trigger_keywords: how does find_largest_icon select among icons
what attribute determines icon size ranking
how is the best favicon chosen from multiple link elements
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: findLargestIcon() selects the icon element whose 'sizes' attribute yields the largest value from parseSize(). If multiple icons tie on size, maxByOrNull picks the first in document order. The result is returned as an absolute URL via 'abs:href'. If the winning element has no href, null is returned and the output is 'url='.

Full Crux web-content toolkit surface: whitespace/title cleaning, substring counting, Google/Facebook redirect rewriting, tracking parameter removal, URL classification with complete media/archive/executable/article extension groups, size parsing and icon selection, image extraction from HTML/CSS/backgrounds, link extraction from DOM subtrees, base-URL absolute resolution, and metadata output formats.

Route by op values clean_whitespace, clean_title, count_matches, rewrite_google, rewrite_facebook, remove_tracking, classify_url, parse_size, find_largest_icon, extract_image, and extract_link. Public cases define exact extension classification, URL rewriting, and output ordering.

The embedded public_test_cases are part of oracle_data and are authoritative for exact input shapes, action/operation names, stdout line order, whitespace, error categories, and deterministic formatting. When prose and expected_output differ, expected_output is the pass-critical contract.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: following well-known link-forwarding interstitials from major platforms
context_pointers.actual_meaning: FacebookUrlRewriter (host ends with .facebook.com, path /l.php, param 'u') and GoogleUrlRewriter (host ends with .google.com, path /url, param 'q' then 'url') in golden/src/main/kotlin/com/chimbori/crux/plugins/

context_pointers.vague_reference: stripping campaign analytics tags from query strings
context_pointers.actual_meaning: TrackingParameterRemover in golden/src/main/kotlin/com/chimbori/crux/plugins/TrackingParameterRemover.kt — removes the 29-entry TRACKING_PARAMETERS array (utm_*, ga_*, fb_*, gs_l, yclid, _openstat, etc.)

context_pointers.vague_reference: parsing icon size declarations to find the best high-resolution favicon
context_pointers.actual_meaning: parseSize() and findLargestIcon() in golden/src/main/kotlin/com/chimbori/crux/extractors/MetadataHelpers.kt — parses 'WxH' size strings and selects the icon element with the largest dimension

context_pointers.vague_reference: checking direct attributes on the root element before descending into the subtree and finally inspecting inline style declarations for background references
context_pointers.actual_meaning: ImageUrlExtractor.findImage() priority order in golden/src/main/kotlin/com/chimbori/crux/extractors/ImageUrlExtractor.kt — src/data-src on root, then img descendants, then any element, then CSS url(...) in style attribute

context_pointers.vague_reference: the I/O contract is a simple line-oriented key-value protocol
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The execution adapter in rcb_tests/dispatcher/Dispatcher.kt reads one JSON object from stdin, dispatches on the 'op' field, and writes 'key=value\n' lines to stdout

context_pointers.vague_reference: classifying web addresses by the type of content they likely point to
context_pointers.actual_meaning: isLikelyVideo(), isLikelyImage(), isLikelyAudio(), isLikelyBinaryDocument(), isLikelyArchive(), isLikelyExecutable(), isLikelyArticle() extension functions in golden/src/main/kotlin/com/chimbori/crux/common/HttpUrlExtensions.kt — all operate on the lowercased file extension of the URL's encoded path

oracle_data.fallback_response: The question does not match any documented constraint. Refer to the dispatcher adapter (rcb_tests/dispatcher/Dispatcher.kt) for the complete op-to-function mapping, the golden/ source tree for core library implementations, and the public test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for concrete input/output examples.

feature1_1_collapse_whitespace.description: Collapse every run of whitespace (spaces, tabs, newlines) inside a string down to a single space and strip leading and trailing whitespace, returning the normalized text.

feature1_2_count_substring.description: Count how many non-overlapping times a substring occurs within a string.

feature1_3_clean_title.description: Clean a raw page title by removing a trailing site-name segment. When a pipe separator appears in the right half of the string, keep only the text before the first pipe (trimmed); otherwise just normalize internal whitespace and leave the title intact.

feature2_classify_url.description: Classify a URL purely by the file extension of its path into media categories. Emit one boolean line per category (video, image, audio, binary_document, archive, executable) plus a final 'article' flag that is true only when the URL matches none of the downloadable-media categories.

cases.expected_output:
video=true
image=false
audio=false
binary_document=false
archive=false
executable=false
article=false

video=false
image=false
audio=false
binary_document=false
archive=false
executable=false
article=true

feature3_1_facebook_rewriter.description: Unwrap a Facebook link-shim URL: when the host ends with .facebook.com and the path is the link interstitial, follow the wrapped target carried in the query, repeating until the result is no longer a Facebook shim. URLs that are not Facebook shims are returned unchanged.

feature3_2_google_rewriter.description: Unwrap a Google link-redirect URL: when the host ends with .google.com and the path is the redirect endpoint, follow the wrapped target carried in the 'q' query parameter (falling back to 'url'), repeating until the result is no longer a Google redirect. URLs that are not Google redirects are returned unchanged.

feature3_3_resolve_redirects.description: Resolve a URL through all known interstitial/redirect providers (Facebook link shims and Google redirect endpoints) repeatedly until the URL stops changing, returning the final destination. A URL that matches no provider is returned unchanged (only normalized by the URL parser).

feature3_4_remove_tracking.description: Strip known analytics/tracking query parameters (e.g. utm_* and similar campaign tags) from a URL while preserving all other query parameters and the rest of the URL. URLs with no tracking parameters are returned unchanged (only normalized by the URL parser).

feature4_1_parse_size.description: Parse an HTML size descriptor string and return the single largest dimension it contains. A descriptor is a 'WxH' pair (case-insensitive 'x'); multiple space-separated pairs are all considered and the largest dimension across them wins. Unparseable or empty input yields 0.

feature4_2_largest_icon.description: Given an HTML fragment (parsed against a base URL) containing icon link elements with optional 'sizes' attributes, select the icon whose declared size has the largest dimension and return its absolute URL.

input.html:
<link rel="icon" sizes="57x57"   href="/57.png">
<link rel="icon" sizes="72x72"   href="/72.png">
<link rel="icon" sizes="114x114" href="/114.png">
<link rel="icon" sizes="144x144" href="/144.png">
<link rel="icon"                 href="/no-size.png">

<link rel="apple-touch-icon-precomposed" sizes="512x512" href="/512.png">
<link rel="apple-touch-icon"             sizes="57x57"   href="/57.png">
<link rel="icon"                         sizes="72x72"   href="/72.png">
<link rel="icon"                         sizes="114x114" href="/114.png">
<link rel="apple-touch-icon"             sizes="144x144" href="/144.png">

feature5_1_extract_image.description: Given an HTML fragment and a base URL, pick the single best representative image URL from the DOM subtree. The search prefers a src/data-src on the root, then on descendant elements, then any element with src/data-src, then a CSS background-image declared in a style attribute. The chosen reference is resolved against the base URL to an absolute URL.

feature5_2_extract_link.description: Given an HTML fragment and a base URL, pick the single best representative link URL from the DOM subtree. The search prefers an href on the root element, then any descendant element carrying an href. The chosen reference is resolved against the base URL to an absolute URL.
